title: CIL System — Full-length Mock 02
pattern: CIL MT — 200 MCQ · 2 papers · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness

questions.solution: Gold

questions.stem: A 20 L can filled with milk. 4 L removed, replaced with water. Process repeated twice. Milk left:

questions.stem: The average of squares of first 10 natural numbers is:

questions.solution: 37.5 days

questions.solution: 400 sec

questions.solution: 7.5 cm

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability

questions.solution: 6 cm

questions.stem: Three successive discounts of 10%, 15%, 20% are equivalent to a single discount of:

questions.solution: 200 m/min

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: Some books are interesting. All interesting things are engaging. Conclusions: I. Some books are engaging. II. All engaging things are interesting.
questions.solution: Only I follows

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Six friends P,Q,R,S,T,U sit around a circle. P is between S and Q. R is opposite P. T is right of R. Who is left of U?
questions.solution: Cannot determine

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man walks 5 km towards north, turns right 12 km, right 5 km, left 3 km. How far from start?
questions.solution: 15 km

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Mirror image of 'PAGE' when mirror is placed on the left:
questions.solution: EGAP

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A married B. Their daughter C married D. E is the son of C. How is B related to E?
questions.solution: Grandmother

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Angle between minute hand and hour hand at 7:40:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: Some A are B. All B are C. Some C are D. Conclusions: I. Some A are C. II. Some D are B.
questions.solution: Only I follows

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A,B,C,D,E,F,G sit in circle. A is 2nd right of B. C is 3rd left of D. E is between F and G. F is opposite A. If B is left of E, who is opposite D?
questions.solution: B

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man faces north. He turns 45° right, then 135° left, then 90° right. Direction now:
questions.solution: East

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If a clock shows 3:30, what time does its mirror image show?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A is the mother of B. C is the husband of B. D is the daughter of C. E is the son of A. How is E related to D?
questions.solution: Uncle

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: At what time between 9 and 10 will hands be in straight line?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: All rings are bells. Some bells are chains. Conclusions: I. Some rings are chains. II. All bells are rings.
questions.solution: Only II follows

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a line, X is 12th from left. Y is 18th from right. If they swap, X becomes 20th from left. Total people:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A ship sails 20 km east, 15 km north, 20 km west, 25 km south. Distance from start:
questions.solution: 10 km south

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: What time in mirror shows 2:15?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A is the father of B. B is the mother of C. C is the husband of D. How is A related to D?
questions.solution: Father-in-law

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a code, 1=A, 2=B, ..., 26=Z. MOBILE = 13-15-2-9-12-5. LAPTOP = ?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a code, 512 means 'you are good', 231 means 'they are bad', 145 means 'good and bad'. Code for 'they' is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Should mining companies be mandated to spend 5% of profit on local community development? Arg I: Yes, mining affects local communities. Arg II: No, it reduces profitability.
questions.solution: Only I is strong

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next: AB, EF, IJ, MN, ?
questions.solution: QR

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If 15 March 2026 is Sunday, what day is 15 August 2026?
questions.solution: Saturday

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: All diamonds are minerals. Some minerals are metals. No metal is a gem. Conclusions: I. Some diamonds are gems. II. Some minerals are not gems.
questions.solution: Only II follows

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A's mother is the sister of B's father. How is A related to B?
questions.solution: Cousin

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next: 2, 5, 10, 17, 26, ?

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of CATALYST:
questions.solution: Barrier

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: Underground mining is one of the most hazardous occupations in the world. The primary risks include roof falls, gas explosions, flooding, and ventilation failures. In India, the Mines Act of 1952 and the Coal Mines Regulations of 2017 govern safety standards. Modern technology has significantly improved mine safety through real-time monitoring systems, gas detectors, and automated equipment. Despite these advances, safety remains a critical concern, and regular training programs are mandatory for all mine workers. The introduction of continuous miners and longwall mining methods has increased both productivity and safety.

The Coal Mines Regulations were enacted in:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of OSTENTATIOUS:
questions.solution: Modest

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: Underground mining is one of the most hazardous occupations in the world. The primary risks include roof falls, gas explosions, flooding, and ventilation failures. In India, the Mines Act of 1952 and the Coal Mines Regulations of 2017 govern safety standards. Modern technology has significantly improved mine safety through real-time monitoring systems, gas detectors, and automated equipment. Despite these advances, safety remains a critical concern, and regular training programs are mandatory for all mine workers. The introduction of continuous miners and longwall mining methods has increased both productivity and safety.

The word 'mandatory' in the passage most nearly means:
questions.solution: Compulsory

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of BENEVOLENCE:
questions.solution: Malevolence

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late 18th century, was powered largely by coal. Steam engines, fuelled by coal, drove factories, railways, and ships, transforming economies and societies. The demand for coal led to the expansion of mining operations across Europe and America. However, the social cost was high, with poor working conditions, child labour, and environmental pollution. The legacy of the Industrial Revolution continues to influence modern industrial practices and environmental policies. Today, many former mining regions are being revitalised through diversification and clean energy initiatives.

Former mining regions are being revitalised through:
questions.solution: Diversification and clean energy initiatives

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of PROCRASTINATE:
questions.solution: Hasten

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: Coal is the most abundant fossil fuel in India and plays a crucial role in the country's energy mix. India has the fourth-largest coal reserves in the world, with an estimated 344 billion tonnes. Coal India Limited (CIL) is the largest coal-producing company globally, accounting for about 82% of India's coal output. However, concerns about environmental degradation and climate change have prompted a shift towards cleaner energy sources like solar and wind power. The government has set a target of achieving 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030.

The government's renewable energy target by 2030 is ____ GW.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of AUDACIOUS:
questions.solution: Meek

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Gondwana coal fields of eastern India contain the country's best quality coal. These fields stretch across Jharkhand, West Bengal, Odisha, and Chhattisgarh. The coal found here is primarily bituminous, which has high calorific value and low ash content. In contrast, the Tertiary coal fields of northeastern India contain lignite and sub-bituminous coal. Coal grading is based on ash content, with higher grades having lower ash percentages. Washeries are used to reduce ash content and improve coal quality before it is supplied to power plants and industries.

Tertiary coal fields of northeastern India contain:
questions.solution: Lignite and sub-bituminous coal

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of FORTIFY:
questions.solution: Weaken

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late 18th century, was powered largely by coal. Steam engines, fuelled by coal, drove factories, railways, and ships, transforming economies and societies. The demand for coal led to the expansion of mining operations across Europe and America. However, the social cost was high, with poor working conditions, child labour, and environmental pollution. The legacy of the Industrial Revolution continues to influence modern industrial practices and environmental policies. Today, many former mining regions are being revitalised through diversification and clean energy initiatives.

What powered steam engines during the Industrial Revolution?
questions.solution: Coal

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of EPHEMERAL:
questions.solution: Eternal

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: India's energy demand is projected to grow at 3-4% annually over the next decade. While coal remains the dominant source, accounting for about 55% of installed capacity, renewable energy is expanding rapidly. Solar power capacity has grown from just 2.6 GW in 2014 to over 70 GW in 2024. Wind power contributes another 45 GW. The government's Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for solar manufacturing aims to reduce dependence on imports. Nuclear power, with 7.5 GW capacity, and hydropower, with 47 GW, are other important components of India's energy portfolio.

The PLI scheme is aimed at:
questions.solution: Reducing dependence on solar imports

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of EMANCIPATE:
questions.solution: Enslave

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: Coal is the most abundant fossil fuel in India and plays a crucial role in the country's energy mix. India has the fourth-largest coal reserves in the world, with an estimated 344 billion tonnes. Coal India Limited (CIL) is the largest coal-producing company globally, accounting for about 82% of India's coal output. However, concerns about environmental degradation and climate change have prompted a shift towards cleaner energy sources like solar and wind power. The government has set a target of achieving 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030.

What percentage of India's coal output does CIL account for?
questions.solution: About 82%

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of MAGNANIMOUS:
questions.solution: Stingy

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: government / new / the / announced / policy / has / a / mining
options: The government has a new mining policy announced
Government has the new mining policy announced
The government has announced a new mining policy
Announced a new mining policy the government has
questions.solution: The government has announced a new mining policy

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The government should ____ heavy penalties for violations.
questions.solution: impose

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The teacher was satisfied ____ the results.
questions.solution: with

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of CONUNDRUM:
questions.solution: Solution

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To take with a grain of salt':
questions.solution: To be skeptical

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of ADMONISH:
questions.solution: Praise

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'A blessing in disguise':
options: A good thing that seemed bad
questions.solution: A good thing that seemed bad

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of FERVENT:
questions.solution: Indifferent

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An orphan process is:
options: A child whose parent has terminated
questions.solution: Orphan = parent died first. Adopted by init (PID 1).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A data word of 16 bits occupies how many bytes?
questions.solution: 16 bits ÷ 8 = 2 bytes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A regular expression represents:
questions.solution: Regular expressions = type-3 grammars = regular languages.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'this' pointer in C++:
options: Points to the current object instance (implicitly passed to member functions)
questions.solution: this: hidden parameter in member functions. *this returns reference to current object.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A multi-tasking OS allows:
options: Only one process at a time
questions.solution: Multi-tasking = concurrent execution (single CPU: time-sharing).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The cache memory is based on the principle of:
questions.solution: Cache exploits temporal (reuse) and spatial (nearby data) locality.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A problem is NP-hard if:
options: Every problem in NP reduces to it in polynomial time
questions.solution: NP-hard: at least as hard as every problem in NP. NP-complete = NP-hard + in NP.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Concepts in C++20:
options: Template constraints (specify requirements on template parameters with readable syntax)
questions.solution: Concepts: template<typename T> concept Integral = is_integral_v<T>;. Requires clause for constraints.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The memory compaction technique:
options: Relocates processes to consolidate free memory into one block
questions.solution: Compaction: move allocated blocks together to reduce external fragmentation (used in segmentation).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An ideal 5-stage pipeline processes 100 instructions. The speedup over a non-pipelined CPU is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The language L = {ww | w ∈ {a,b}*} is:
options: Not context-free (requires comparison of two equal halves)
questions.solution: ww: requires comparing first half with second half. Not CFL (pumping lemma for CFL).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A variable declared as 'static' inside a C function:
options: Retains its value between function calls
questions.solution: Static local: lifetime = program duration, scope = function only.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Processor affinity binds:
options: A process to a memory region
A process to a specific CPU (improves cache performance)
questions.solution: Affinity: soft (OS tries but may migrate) or hard (pinned to specific CPU).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The instruction register (IR) holds:
questions.solution: IR: stores instruction fetched from memory for decoding and execution.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A non-deterministic TM is:
options: Equivalent in power to a deterministic TM
questions.solution: NDTM = DTM in power (exponential slowdown for simulation).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pure virtual function is declared as:
questions.solution: Pure virtual: class becomes abstract (cannot instantiate). Derived classes must override.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'mount' command:
options: Attaches a filesystem to a directory (mount point)
questions.solution: mount: mount -t ext4 /dev/sda1 /mnt. umount: unmount. /etc/fstab: auto-mount at boot.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Polled I/O:
options: The CPU repeatedly checks device status (busy-waiting)
questions.solution: Polling: CPU actively checks if device is ready. Wastes CPU cycles if device is slow.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inherently ambiguous language:
options: Every grammar for the language is ambiguous
questions.solution: Inherently ambiguous: e.g., {a^n b^n c^m} ∪ {a^n b^m c^m}. All grammars are ambiguous.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Variable-length array (VLA) in C99:
options: Allows arrays whose size is determined at runtime (automatic storage)
Allows runtime size with static storage
questions.solution: VLA: int arr[n]; where n is variable. Not supported in standard C++ (but gcc extension).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which condition is NOT one of the four necessary conditions for deadlock?
questions.solution: Deadlock needs mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, NO preemption, and circular wait — so 'preemption' is not a condition.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Amdahl's law states:
options: Speedup ≤ 1 / ((1 - P) + P / N) where P = parallelizable fraction
questions.solution: Amdahl's law: even infinite processors can't speed up the sequential portion. Gustafson's law is more optimistic.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: If P ≠ NP, then:
options: All NP problems are in P
There exist problems in NP that are not in P
questions.solution: P ≠ NP: some NP problems have no polynomial-time solution. Widely believed true.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The time complexity of an algorithm is O(1) meaning:
questions.solution: O(1) = constant time (e.g., array access by index).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about virtual memory is INCORRECT?
options: The page table maps pages to frames
Virtual memory can exceed physical RAM
Page faults are handled entirely by the CPU without disk access
A page fault may load a page from disk
questions.solution: A page fault for a non-resident page requires the OS to fetch it from disk.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The CPU control unit:
options: Generates control signals to coordinate CPU operations
questions.solution: Control unit: hardwired (fast, fixed) or microprogrammed (flexible).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Post Correspondence Problem (PCP):
options: Is undecidable (determine if sequence of dominos matches)
questions.solution: PCP: given dominoes (x_i, y_i), find sequence where concatenated x = concatenated y. Undecidable.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inline functions in C++:
options: Suggest the compiler to replace the function call with the function body (avoid function call overhead)
questions.solution: inline: hint to compiler (may be ignored). Reduces call overhead but may increase code size.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The seek time of a disk is:
options: Time to rotate to the correct sector
Time to move the read/write head to the correct track
questions.solution: Seek = head movement. Rotational latency = rotation to sector. Transfer = data read.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Pipelining improves:
questions.solution: Pipeline: multiple instructions at different stages. Ideal CPI = 1 (vs 4-5 for non-pipelined).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An NFA differs from a DFA because:
options: An NFA can have multiple transitions for the same input symbol
questions.solution: NFA: multiple possible next states (including ε-transitions). DFA: exactly one per symbol.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A stack overflow occurs when:
options: Recursion depth exceeds the available stack memory
questions.solution: Excessive recursion causes stack overflow (each call pushes to stack).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The /sys filesystem:
options: Exposes kernel objects (devices, drivers, buses) in a structured tree
questions.solution: /sys: sysfs. /sys/class/net, /sys/block, /sys/devices. More structured than /proc.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 20 ns, memory access = 200 ns, hit ratio = 0.9. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A grammar with production S → aS | ε generates:
options: a* (any number of a's including empty)
questions.solution: S → ε (empty) and S → aS (recursion) generates a*.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The offsetof macro:
options: Returns the byte offset of a member within a struct type
Returns the alignment of a member
Returns the address of a member
Returns the size of a member
questions.solution: offsetof(type, member): from <stddef.h>. Useful for manual serialization and container_of.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Journaling file systems (ext3/4) improve:
questions.solution: Journal: log metadata before writing to disk → fast recovery on crash.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Hamming code provides:
options: Single-bit error correction and double-bit detection
questions.solution: Hamming: SECDED (Single Error Correct, Double Error Detect) with extra parity bits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Rice-Shapiro theorem:
options: Characterizes the index sets that are c.e. (recursively enumerable)
questions.solution: Rice-Shapiro: refinement of Rice's theorem for c.e. index sets. Deals with monotonic properties.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An iterator in C++:
options: Provides a way to access elements of a container sequentially (similar to pointer)
questions.solution: Iterator: input, output, forward, bidirectional, random-access categories.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 100 MB at 250 MB/s takes about:
questions.solution: time = size/rate = 100/250 = 0.4 s.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: FMA (Fused Multiply-Add) does:
options: a + b × c with double rounding
a × b + c in one instruction (single rounding)
questions.solution: FMA: single rounding step (more accurate than multiply then add). FMA3 (Intel Haswell+), FMA4 (AMD).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An NFA with ε-transitions:
options: Can transition without consuming any input symbol
questions.solution: ε-NFA: ε-moves do not add power (can be converted to DFA).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pointer variable stores:
options: The memory address of another variable
questions.solution: Pointers hold addresses, enabling indirect access and dynamic structures.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The critical section problem requires:
questions.solution: Three conditions: mutual exclusion, progress, bounded waiting.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache mapping techniques include:
questions.solution: Three types: direct mapping, fully associative, set-associative (k-way).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Busy Beaver function Σ(n):
options: Is uncomputable (maximum ones a halting n-state TM can print)
questions.solution: Busy Beaver: Σ(1)=1, Σ(2)=4, Σ(3)=6, Σ(4)=13, Σ(5)≥4098 (unknown). Uncomputable function.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The setjmp/longjmp mechanism:
options: Provides non-local gotos (jump across function calls, similar to exceptions)
questions.solution: setjmp(jmp_buf): saves stack context. longjmp(jmp_buf, val): restores context (jumps back to setjmp).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The buddy system allocates memory in:
questions.solution: Buddy system = split/coalesce in powers of 2 (internal fragmentation).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: VLIW stands for:
questions.solution: VLIW: compiler packs multiple operations into one long instruction word.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Chomsky hierarchy consists of:
questions.solution: Chomsky hierarchy: 0 (TM) ⊃ 1 (LBA) ⊃ 2 (PDA) ⊃ 3 (DFA/NFA).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Perfect forwarding in C++:
options: Preserves value category (lvalue/rvalue) when forwarding arguments to another function
questions.solution: Perfect forwarding: template<typename T> void f(T&& t) { g(std::forward<T>(t)); }. Reference collapsing rules.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A file system inode contains:
options: Metadata (permissions, size, timestamps, block pointers) not the filename
questions.solution: Inode: stores attributes + 15 block pointers (12 direct + 1 indirect + 1 double + 1 triple in ext2/3).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Dennard scaling:
options: As transistors shrink, power density stays constant (voltage and current scale with dimensions)
questions.solution: Dennard scaling (1974): voltage ∝ 1/k, current ∝ 1/k → power/area stays constant. Ended ~2006 (Dark Silicon).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Ambiguous grammar:
options: Produces more than one parse tree for some string
questions.solution: Ambiguity: different leftmost/rightmost derivations → different parse trees. E.g., dangling else.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Smart pointer unique_ptr:
options: Owns the pointed object exclusively (cannot be copied, only moved)
questions.solution: unique_ptr: exclusive ownership (move-only). shared_ptr: shared ownership (reference counting).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A process in the 'ready' state:
options: Is loaded in main memory and waiting for CPU
questions.solution: Ready = in memory, waiting for CPU scheduling.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A data word of 24 bits occupies how many bytes?
questions.solution: 24 bits ÷ 8 = 3 bytes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The demultiplexer (DEMUX) does:
options: Routes one input to one of many outputs based on select lines
Combines multiple inputs to one output
questions.solution: DEMUX = reverse of MUX: 1 input, 2^n outputs, n selects.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Round-Robin scheduling with quantum q leads to:
options: n processes each getting 1/n of CPU time (fair)
questions.solution: RR = time-sharing; each process gets CPU up to q time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The BCD code for decimal 25 is:
questions.solution: BCD = 4 bits per digit. 2 = 0010, 5 = 0101.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The time complexity of Kruskal's algorithm is:
options: O(E log E) or O(E log V)
questions.solution: Kruskal: sort E edges = O(E log E), union-find ~ O(α(V)).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the NAND gate shown with inputs A = 0 and B = 0, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=0</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=0</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">NAND</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: NAND gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Checkpoint in database recovery:
options: Flushes dirty pages and records LSN in log (reduces recovery time after crash)
questions.solution: Checkpoint: periodic consistency point. Fuzzy checkpoint doesn't stop transactions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The cache tag stores:
options: The upper bits of the memory address (to identify which block is cached)
questions.solution: Tag: compared to determine cache hit (along with valid bit).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The SELECT statement is used for:
questions.solution: SELECT = query/retrieval. INSERT = add rows. DELETE = remove rows. UPDATE = modify rows.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The binary equivalent of decimal 37 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The longest increasing subsequence (LIS) can be solved in:
options: O(n log n) using patience sorting (binary search on tails)
questions.solution: LIS: maintain tails[i] = min possible tail of increasing subsequence of length i. Binary search each element.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With a page size of 8 KB, the number of bits used for the page offset is:
questions.solution: 8KB = 2^13 bytes, so the offset field is 13 bits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The TCP connection is established using a:
questions.solution: TCP setup uses SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK; teardown uses a four-way exchange.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The time complexity of binary search for n=128 is:
questions.solution: Binary search halves the search space each step: O(log n) ≈ O(log 128).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Naive recursive Fibonacci fib(6) (with fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1) makes how many calls to the base cases fib(0) or fib(1)?
questions.solution: The number of base-case calls for fib(n) equals fib(n+1) = 13, illustrating exponential blow-up.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Dijkstra's algorithm requires:
questions.solution: Dijkstra: non-negative weights. Bellman-Ford handles negative weights.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which comparison of sorting algorithms is correct?
options: Merge sort is stable; standard quicksort is not
Quicksort is stable; merge sort is not
questions.solution: Merge sort preserves equal-key order (stable); typical quicksort does not.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The number of nodes in a complete binary tree of height h=5 (root at 0) is at most:
questions.solution: Max nodes in complete binary tree of height h = 2^(h+1)-1 = 63.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The space complexity of merge sort is:
questions.solution: Mergesort needs O(n) auxiliary space for merging.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case time complexity of quicksort is:
questions.solution: With consistently poor pivots (e.g. already-sorted input) quicksort degrades to O(n²).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A monitor is a:
options: High-level synchronization construct with condition variables
questions.solution: Monitor = language-level construct for mutual exclusion + condition variables.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The longest common subsequence (LCS) DP requires:
options: O(mn) time and O(mn) space (can optimize to O(min(m,n)) space)
questions.solution: LCS DP: 2D table for prefixes. O(mn) time. Hirschberg's algorithm uses divide-and-conquer for O(min(m,n)) space.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The TCP/IP model has how many layers?
questions.solution: TCP/IP: Network Interface, Internet, Transport, Application.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Prim's algorithm builds MST by:
options: Growing a single tree (adds the cheapest edge connecting tree to non-tree vertex)
questions.solution: Prim: O(E log V) with heap. Grows from a start vertex (like Dijkstra but for undirected graphs).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 5, push 9, push 20, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">21</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">5</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A queue uses which principle?
questions.solution: Queue = FIFO; Stack = LIFO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Functional requirements specify:
options: What the system should do (features, functions)
questions.solution: Functional = behavior/features. Non-functional = quality attributes (performance, security, etc.).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Write-through cache writes to:
options: Both cache and main memory simultaneously
questions.solution: Write-through: consistent but slow (each write goes to RAM). Write-back: only on eviction.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A subquery in SQL is:
options: A query nested inside another query
questions.solution: Subquery (inner query) = nested SELECT inside outer SELECT/FROM/WHERE.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 3, push 9, push 12, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 12 then pop (removes 12), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">3</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A foreign key:
options: References the primary key of another table
questions.solution: Foreign key = column(s) referencing PK of another table (referential integrity).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 9, push 12, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 12 then pop (removes 12), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">7</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A view in SQL is:
options: A virtual table based on the result of a stored query
questions.solution: View: saved query (not data). Updatable views have restrictions. Materialized view = physical copy.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: RISC stands for:
questions.solution: RISC: simple instructions, fixed length, load-store architecture.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Telnet runs on port:
questions.solution: SSH = 22, Telnet = 23, FTP = 21, SMTP = 25, DNS = 53, HTTP = 80, HTTPS = 443.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The language {a^n b^n | n ≥ 0} is:
questions.solution: a^n b^n requires counting → CFG (S → aSb | ε). Not regular (pumping lemma).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A queue implemented with two stacks has enqueue complexity:
questions.solution: Enqueue = push to stack1 (O(1)). Dequeue = if stack2 empty, pop all stack1 → stack2.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With a page size of 2 KB, the number of bits used for the page offset is:
questions.solution: 2KB = 2^11 bytes, so the offset field is 11 bits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum data rate of USB 3.0 is:
questions.solution: USB 2.0 = 480 Mbps. USB 3.0 = 5 Gbps. USB 3.1 Gen 2 = 10 Gbps. USB 3.2 = 20 Gbps.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A gateway operates at:
options: Multiple layers (can translate between different protocols)
questions.solution: Gateway: protocol translator (e.g., email gateway, IoT gateway). Can work at any layer.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Symbolic links:
options: Can only link files in same directory
Store the path to the target file (separate inode, can cross filesystems)
questions.solution: Symlink: ln -s target link_name. Dangling if target deleted. Separate inode with target path.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Write allocate (fetch-on-write):
options: Writes directly to memory on miss
Loads the cache line on a write miss before writing
questions.solution: Write-allocate: good for write-back (subsequent writes hit). No-write-allocate: for write-through.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A link of 100 Mbps has a round-trip time of 20 ms. The bandwidth-delay product is about:
questions.solution: BDP = bandwidth×delay = 100Mbps×20ms ÷ 8 ≈ 244.14 KB.
